## Orbit and rotation

### Rotation

Main article: [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/EpicEarth-Globespin-tilt-23.4.gif/250px-EpicEarth-Globespin-tilt-23.4.gif)](/wiki/File:EpicEarth-Globespin-tilt-23.4.gif)

Satellite [time lapse imagery](/wiki/Time-lapse_photography "Time-lapse photography") of Earth's rotation showing axis tilt

Earth's rotation period relative to the Sun—its mean solar day—is 86,400 seconds of mean solar time (86,400.0025 [SI](/wiki/SI "SI") seconds).[[184]](#cite_note-aj136_5_1906-193) Because Earth's solar day is now slightly longer than it was during the 19th century due to [tidal deceleration](/wiki/Tidal_acceleration "Tidal acceleration"), each day varies between 0 and 2 [ms](/wiki/Millisecond "Millisecond") longer than the mean solar day.[[185]](#cite_note-USNO_TSD-194)[[186]](#cite_note-195)

Earth's rotation period relative to the [fixed stars](/wiki/Fixed_star "Fixed star"), called its *stellar day* by the [International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service](/wiki/International_Earth_Rotation_and_Reference_Systems_Service "International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service") (IERS), is 86,164.0989 seconds of mean solar time ([UT1](/wiki/UT1 "UT1")), or 23h 56m 4.0989s.[[2]](#cite_note-IERS-4)[[n 10]](#cite_note-Aoki-196) Earth's rotation period relative to the [precessing](/wiki/Precession_(astronomy) "Precession (astronomy)") or moving mean [March equinox](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox") (when the Sun is at 90° on the equator), is 86,164.0905 seconds of mean solar time (UT1) (23h 56m 4.0905s).[[2]](#cite_note-IERS-4) Thus the sidereal day is shorter than the stellar day by about 8.4 ms.[[187]](#cite_note-seidelmann1992-197)

Apart from meteors within the atmosphere and low-orbiting satellites, the main apparent motion of celestial bodies in Earth's sky is to the west at a rate of 15°/h = 15'/min. For bodies near the [celestial equator](/wiki/Celestial_equator "Celestial equator"), this is equivalent to an apparent diameter of the Sun or the Moon every two minutes; from Earth's surface, the apparent sizes of the Sun and the Moon are approximately the same.[[188]](#cite_note-zeilik1998-198)[[189]](#cite_note-angular-199)

### Orbit and location

Main articles: [Earth's orbit](/wiki/Earth%27s_orbit "Earth's orbit") and [Earth's location](/wiki/Earth%27s_location "Earth's location")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Seasons1.svg/250px-Seasons1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Seasons1.svg)

Exaggerated illustration of Earth's elliptical orbit around the Sun, marking that the orbital extreme points ([apoapsis](/wiki/Apoapsis "Apoapsis") and [periapsis](/wiki/Periapsis "Periapsis")) are not the same as the four seasonal extreme points, the [equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") and [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice")

Earth orbits the Sun at an average distance is about 150 million km (93 million mi). This makes Earth the third-closest planet to the Sun and part of the [inner Solar System](/wiki/Inner_Solar_System "Inner Solar System"). Its orbital distance is the basis for the [astronomical unit](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (AU) and is equal to roughly 8.3 [light minutes](/wiki/Light_minute "Light minute") or 380 times [Earth's distance to the Moon](/wiki/Lunar_distance "Lunar distance"). Earth orbits the Sun every 365.2564 mean [solar days](/wiki/Solar_day "Solar day"), or one [sidereal year](/wiki/Sidereal_year "Sidereal year"). With an apparent movement of the Sun in Earth's sky at a rate of about 1°/day eastward, which is one apparent Sun or Moon diameter every 12 hours. Due to this motion, on average it takes 24 hours—a solar day—for Earth to complete a full rotation about its axis so that the Sun returns to the [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(astronomy) "Meridian (astronomy)"), slightly longer than the sidereal day.

The orbital speed of Earth averages at 29.7827 km/s (107,218 km/h; 66,622 mph), which is fast enough to travel a distance equal to Earth's diameter, about 12,742 km (7,918 mi), in seven minutes, and the distance from Earth to the Moon, 384,400 km (238,855 mi), in about 3.5 hours.[[3]](#cite_note-earth_fact_sheet-5) Viewed from a vantage point above the Sun and Earth's north poles, Earth orbits in a counterclockwise direction about the Sun. Earth, along with all of the other planets in the Solar System, orbits roughly in the same flat plane, known as the [invariable plane](/wiki/Invariable_plane "Invariable plane"). Earth's inclination to this plane is about 1.58°.[[4]](#cite_note-Allen294-6)

Earth, along with rest of the Solar System, is situated in the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") and orbits about 28,000 [light-years](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") from its center. It is about 20 light-years above the [galactic plane](/wiki/Galactic_plane "Galactic plane") in the [Orion Arm](/wiki/Orion_Arm "Orion Arm").[[190]](#cite_note-nasa20051201-200)

### Axial tilt and seasons

Main article: [Axial tilt § Earth](/wiki/Axial_tilt#Earth "Axial tilt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Axial_tilt_vs_tropical_and_polar_circles.svg/250px-Axial_tilt_vs_tropical_and_polar_circles.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Axial_tilt_vs_tropical_and_polar_circles.svg)

Earth's axial tilt causing different angles of seasonal illumination at different orbital positions around the Sun

The axial tilt of Earth is approximately 23.439281°[[2]](#cite_note-IERS-4) with the axis of the plane of [Earth's orbit](/wiki/Earth%27s_orbit "Earth's orbit") by definition pointing always towards the [Celestial Poles](/wiki/Celestial_Poles "Celestial Poles"). Due to Earth's axial tilt, the amount of sunlight reaching any given point on the surface varies over the course of the year. This causes the seasonal change in climate, with summer in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") occurring when the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") is facing the Sun, and in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") when the [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn") faces the Sun. In each instance, winter occurs simultaneously in the opposite hemisphere.

During the summer, the day lasts longer, and the Sun climbs higher in the sky. In winter, the climate becomes cooler and the days shorter.[[191]](#cite_note-201) Above the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle") and below the [Antarctic Circle](/wiki/Antarctic_Circle "Antarctic Circle") there is no daylight at all for part of the year, causing a [polar night](/wiki/Polar_night "Polar night"), and this night extends for several months at the poles themselves. These same latitudes also experience a [midnight sun](/wiki/Midnight_sun "Midnight sun"), where the sun remains visible all day.[[192]](#cite_note-202)[[193]](#cite_note-203)

By astronomical convention, the four seasons can be determined by the solstices—the points in the orbit of maximum axial tilt toward or away from the Sun—and the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox"), when Earth's rotational axis is aligned with its orbital axis. In the Northern Hemisphere, [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") currently occurs around 21 December; [summer solstice](/wiki/Summer_solstice "Summer solstice") is near 21 June, spring equinox is around 20 March and [autumnal equinox](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox") is about 22 or 23 September. In the Southern Hemisphere, the situation is reversed, with the summer and winter solstices exchanged and the spring and autumnal equinox dates swapped.[[194]](#cite_note-bromberg2008-204)

The angle of Earth's axial tilt is relatively stable over long periods of time. Its axial tilt does undergo [nutation](/wiki/Astronomical_nutation "Astronomical nutation"); a slight, irregular motion with a main period of 18.6 years.[[195]](#cite_note-lin2006-205) The orientation (rather than the angle) of Earth's axis also changes over time, [precessing](/wiki/Axial_precession "Axial precession") around in a complete circle over each 25,800-year cycle; this precession is the reason for the difference between a sidereal year and a [tropical year](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year"). Both of these motions are caused by the varying attraction of the Sun and the Moon on Earth's equatorial bulge. The poles also migrate a few meters across Earth's surface. This [polar motion](/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion") has multiple, cyclical components, which collectively are termed [quasiperiodic motion](/wiki/Quasiperiodic_motion "Quasiperiodic motion"). In addition to an annual component to this motion, there is a 14-month cycle called the [Chandler wobble](/wiki/Chandler_wobble "Chandler wobble"). Earth's rotational velocity also varies in a phenomenon known as length-of-day variation.[[196]](#cite_note-fisher19960205-206)

Earth's orbit is slightly elliptical rather than perfectly circular, and its closest approach to the Sun is called [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion"). In modern times, Earth's perihelion occurs around 3 January, and its [aphelion](/wiki/Aphelion "Aphelion") around 4 July. These dates shift over time due to precession and changes to the orbit, the latter of which follows cyclical patterns known as [Milankovitch cycles](/wiki/Milankovitch_cycles "Milankovitch cycles"). The annual change in the Earth–Sun distance causes an increase of about 6.8% in solar energy reaching Earth at perihelion relative to aphelion.[[197]](#cite_note-207)[[n 11]](#cite_note-solar_energy-208) Because the Southern Hemisphere is tilted toward the Sun at about the same time that Earth reaches the closest approach to the Sun, the Southern Hemisphere receives slightly more energy from the Sun than does the northern over the course of a year. This effect is much less significant than the total energy change due to the axial tilt, and most of the excess energy is absorbed by the higher proportion of water in the Southern Hemisphere.[[198]](#cite_note-209)

## Gravitational domain and influence

Further information: [Satellite system (astronomy)](/wiki/Satellite_system_(astronomy) "Satellite system (astronomy)")

### Gravitational field

Main article: [Gravity of Earth](/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth "Gravity of Earth")

The gravity of Earth is the [acceleration](/wiki/Acceleration "Acceleration") that is imparted to objects due to the distribution of mass within Earth. Near Earth's surface, [gravitational acceleration](/wiki/Gravitational_acceleration "Gravitational acceleration") is approximately 9.8 m/s2 (32 ft/s2). Local differences in topography, geology, and deeper tectonic structure cause local and broad regional differences in Earth's gravitational field, known as [gravity anomalies](/wiki/Gravity_anomaly "Gravity anomaly").[[199]](#cite_note-210) The [Hill sphere](/wiki/Hill_sphere "Hill sphere"), or the [sphere of gravitational influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence_(astrodynamics) "Sphere of influence (astrodynamics)"), of Earth is about 1.5 million km (930,000 mi) in radius.[[200]](#cite_note-vazquez_etal2006-211)[[n 12]](#cite_note-hill_radius-212) This is the maximum distance at which Earth's gravitational influence is stronger than that of the more distant Sun and planets. Objects must orbit Earth within this radius, or they can become unbound by the gravitational perturbation of the Sun.[[200]](#cite_note-vazquez_etal2006-211)

### Moon

Main articles: [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), [Lunar theory](/wiki/Lunar_theory "Lunar theory"), and [Orbit of the Moon](/wiki/Orbit_of_the_Moon "Orbit of the Moon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/MarsReconnaissanceOrbiter-Views-EarthMoon-20220422.jpg/250px-MarsReconnaissanceOrbiter-Views-EarthMoon-20220422.jpg)](/wiki/File:MarsReconnaissanceOrbiter-Views-EarthMoon-20220422.jpg)

Earth and the Moon as seen from [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") by the *[Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter](/wiki/Mars_Reconnaissance_Orbiter "Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter")*

The Moon is a relatively large, [terrestrial](/wiki/Terrestrial_planet "Terrestrial planet"), [planet-like natural satellite](/wiki/Planetary-mass_moon "Planetary-mass moon"), with a diameter about one-quarter of Earth's. It is the largest moon in the Solar System relative to the size of its planet, although [Charon](/wiki/Charon_(moon) "Charon (moon)") is larger relative to the dwarf planet [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto").[[201]](#cite_note-213)[[202]](#cite_note-214) The natural satellites of other planets are also referred to as "moons", after Earth's.[[203]](#cite_note-215) The Moon and Earth orbit a common [barycenter](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") every 27.32 days relative to the background stars. When combined with the Earth–Moon system's common orbit around the Sun, the period of the [synodic month](/wiki/Synodic_month "Synodic month"), from new moon to new moon, is 29.53 days.

Viewed from the [celestial north pole](/wiki/Celestial_pole "Celestial pole"), the motion of Earth, the Moon, and their axial rotations are all [counterclockwise](/wiki/Counterclockwise "Counterclockwise"). Viewed from Earth, the Moon is just far enough away to have almost the same apparent-sized disk as the Sun. The [angular size](/wiki/Angular_size "Angular size") (or [solid angle](/wiki/Solid_angle "Solid angle")) of these two bodies match because, although the Sun's diameter is about 400 times as large as the Moon's, it is also 400 times more distant.[[189]](#cite_note-angular-199) This allows total and annular solar eclipses to occur on Earth.[[204]](#cite_note-216) The orbital planes are not precisely aligned: the Earth-Moon plane is tilted up to ±5.1 degrees against the Earth–Sun plane (the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic")). Without this tilt, there would be an eclipse every two weeks, alternating between [lunar eclipses](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") and [solar eclipses](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse").[[3]](#cite_note-earth_fact_sheet-5)[[205]](#cite_note-moon_fact_sheet-217)

The gravitational attraction between Earth and the Moon causes [lunar tides](/wiki/Lunar_tide "Lunar tide") on Earth.[[206]](#cite_note-218) The same effect on the Moon has led to its [tidal locking](/wiki/Tidal_locking "Tidal locking"): its rotation period is the same as the time it takes to orbit Earth. As a result, it always presents the same face to the planet.[[207]](#cite_note-219) As the Moon orbits Earth, different parts of its face are illuminated by the Sun, leading to the [lunar phases](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase").[[208]](#cite_note-220) Due to their [tidal interaction](/wiki/Tidal_interactions "Tidal interactions"), the Moon recedes from Earth at the rate of approximately 38 mm/a (1+1⁄2 in/year). Over millions of years, these tiny modifications—and the lengthening of Earth's day by about 23 [μs](/wiki/Microsecond "Microsecond")/yr—add up to significant changes.[[209]](#cite_note-espenak_meeus20070207-221) During the [Ediacaran](/wiki/Ediacaran "Ediacaran") period, for example, (approximately 620 Ma) there were 400±7 days in a year, with each day lasting 21.9±0.4 hours.[[210]](#cite_note-222)

The Moon may have dramatically affected the development of life by moderating the planet's climate. [Paleontological](/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology") evidence and computer simulations show that Earth's axial tilt is stabilized by tidal interactions with the Moon.[[211]](#cite_note-aaa428_261-223) Some theorists think that without this stabilization against the [torques](/wiki/Torque "Torque") applied by the Sun and planets to Earth's equatorial bulge, the rotational axis might be chaotically unstable, exhibiting large changes over millions of years, as is the case for Mars, though this is disputed.[[212]](#cite_note-224)[[213]](#cite_note-225)

The most widely accepted theory of the Moon's origin, the [giant-impact hypothesis](/wiki/Giant-impact_hypothesis "Giant-impact hypothesis"), states that it formed from the collision of a Mars-size protoplanet called Theia with the early Earth. This hypothesis explains the Moon's relative lack of iron and volatile elements and the fact that its composition is nearly identical to that of Earth's crust.[[38]](#cite_note-canup_asphaug2001b-43) Computer simulations suggest that two blob-like remnants of this protoplanet could be inside the Earth.[[214]](#cite_note-NYT-20231101-226)[[215]](#cite_note-NAT-20231101-227)

### Asteroids and artificial satellites

Main articles: [Near-Earth object](/wiki/Near-Earth_object "Near-Earth object") and [Claimed moons of Earth](/wiki/Claimed_moons_of_Earth "Claimed moons of Earth")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Debris-GEO1280.jpg/250px-Debris-GEO1280.jpg)](/wiki/File:Debris-GEO1280.jpg)

A computer-generated image mapping the prevalence of [artificial satellites](/wiki/Artificial_satellite "Artificial satellite") and [space debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris") around Earth in [geosynchronous](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") and [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit")

Earth's [co-orbital asteroids](/wiki/Co-orbital_configuration "Co-orbital configuration") population consists of [quasi-satellites](/wiki/Quasi-satellite "Quasi-satellite"), objects with a [horseshoe orbit](/wiki/Horseshoe_orbit "Horseshoe orbit") and [trojans](/wiki/Trojan_(celestial_body) "Trojan (celestial body)"). There are at least seven quasi-satellites, including [469219 Kamoʻoalewa](/wiki/469219_Kamo%CA%BBoalewa "469219 Kamoʻoalewa"), ranging in diameter from 10 m to 5000 m.[[216]](#cite_note-christou_asher2011-228)[[217]](#cite_note-229) A [trojan asteroid](/wiki/Earth_trojan "Earth trojan") companion, [2010 TK7](/wiki/2010_TK7 "2010 TK7"), is [librating](/wiki/Libration "Libration") around the leading [Lagrange triangular point](/wiki/Lagrangian_point "Lagrangian point"), L4, in [Earth's orbit](/wiki/Earth%27s_orbit "Earth's orbit") around the Sun.[[218]](#cite_note-Choi-230) The tiny [near-Earth asteroid](/wiki/Near-Earth_asteroid "Near-Earth asteroid") [2006 RH120](/wiki/2006_RH120 "2006 RH120") makes close approaches to the Earth–Moon system roughly every twenty years. During these approaches, it can orbit Earth for brief periods of time.[[219]](#cite_note-231)

As of September 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Earth&action=edit), there are 4,550 operational, human-made [satellites](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") orbiting Earth.[[220]](#cite_note-ucs-232) There are also inoperative satellites, including [Vanguard 1](/wiki/Vanguard_1 "Vanguard 1"), the oldest satellite currently in orbit, and over 16,000 pieces of tracked [space debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris").[[n 13]](#cite_note-space_debris-233) Earth's largest artificial satellite is the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") (ISS).[[221]](#cite_note-234)

## Life on Earth

Main articles: [Biosphere](/wiki/Biosphere "Biosphere") and [History of life](/wiki/History_of_life "History of life")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Mollweide_Cycle.gif/250px-Mollweide_Cycle.gif)](/wiki/File:Mollweide_Cycle.gif)

An animation of the changing density of [productive](/wiki/Primary_production "Primary production") vegetation on land (low in brown; heavy in dark green) and phytoplankton at the [ocean surface](/wiki/Ocean_surface "Ocean surface") (low in purple; high in yellow)

Earth is the only known place that has ever been [habitable](/wiki/Planetary_habitability "Planetary habitability") for life. Earth's life developed in Earth's early bodies of water some hundred million years after Earth formed, roughly 4 billion years ago. Earth provides liquid water—an environment where complex [organic molecules](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") can assemble and interact, and sufficient energy to sustain a [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism").[[222]](#cite_note-ab2003-235) Plants and other organisms take up [nutrients](/wiki/Nutrient "Nutrient") from water, soils and the atmosphere. These nutrients are constantly recycled between different species.[[223]](#cite_note-236)

Earth's life has been shaping and inhabiting many particular [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") on Earth and has eventually expanded globally forming an overarching biosphere.[[224]](#cite_note-237) Earth's life has also over time greatly diversified, allowing the biosphere to have different [biomes](/wiki/Biome "Biome"), which are inhabited by comparatively similar plants and animals.[[225]](#cite_note-238) The different biomes developed at distinct elevations or [water depths](/wiki/Ocean_depths "Ocean depths"), planetary temperature [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and on land also with different [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity"). [Earth's species diversity](/wiki/Latitudinal_gradients_in_species_diversity "Latitudinal gradients in species diversity") and [biomass](/wiki/Biomass_(ecology) "Biomass (ecology)") reaches a peak in shallow waters and with [forests, particularly in equatorial, warm and humid conditions](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest"). While freezing [polar regions](/wiki/Polar_regions_of_Earth "Polar regions of Earth") and [high altitudes](/wiki/Alpine_tundra "Alpine tundra"), or [extremely arid areas](/wiki/Desert "Desert") are relatively barren of plant and animal life.[[226]](#cite_note-amnat163_2_192-239)

Therefore, life has impacted Earth, significantly altering Earth's atmosphere and surface over long periods of time, causing changes like the [Great Oxidation Event](/wiki/Great_Oxidation_Event "Great Oxidation Event").[[227]](#cite_note-240) Humans furthermore have impacted Earth, its life and development.

### Origin of life and evolution

Main articles: [Abiogenesis](/wiki/Abiogenesis "Abiogenesis"), [Earliest known life forms](/wiki/Earliest_known_life_forms "Earliest known life forms"), and [History of life](/wiki/History_of_life "History of life")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Archean.png/250px-Archean.png)](/wiki/File:Archean.png)

An impression of the [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean"), the [eon](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale#Divisions_of_geologic_time "Geologic time scale") after Earth's formation, featuring round [stromatolites](/wiki/Stromatolite "Stromatolite"), which are early oxygen-producing forms of life from billions of years ago. After the [Late Heavy Bombardment](/wiki/Late_Heavy_Bombardment "Late Heavy Bombardment"), [Earth's crust](/wiki/Earth%27s_crust "Earth's crust") had cooled, its water-rich barren [surface](/wiki/Planetary_surface "Planetary surface") is marked by [continents](/wiki/Continent "Continent") and [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"), with the Moon still orbiting Earth half as far as it is today, appearing 2.8 times larger and producing strong [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide").[[228]](#cite_note-Lunar_and_Planetary_Institute-241)

[Chemical reactions](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") led to the first self-replicating molecules about four billion years ago. A half billion years later, the [last common ancestor of all current life](/wiki/Last_universal_common_ancestor "Last universal common ancestor") arose.[[229]](#cite_note-sa282_6_90-242) The evolution of [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis") allowed the Sun's energy to be harvested directly by life forms. The resultant [molecular oxygen](/wiki/Molecular_oxygen "Molecular oxygen") (O2) accumulated in the atmosphere and due to interaction with ultraviolet solar radiation, formed a protective [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer") (O3) in the upper atmosphere.[[156]](#cite_note-NYT-20131003-165) The incorporation of smaller cells within larger ones resulted in the [development of complex cells](/wiki/Endosymbiotic_theory "Endosymbiotic theory") called [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote").[[230]](#cite_note-jas22_3_225-243) True multicellular organisms formed as cells within [colonies](/wiki/Colony_(biology) "Colony (biology)") became increasingly specialized. Aided by the absorption of harmful [ultraviolet radiation](/wiki/Ultraviolet_radiation "Ultraviolet radiation") by the ozone layer, life colonized Earth's surface.[[231]](#cite_note-burton20021129-244) Among the earliest [fossil](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") evidence for life is [microbial mat](/wiki/Microbial_mat "Microbial mat") fossils found in 3.48 billion-year-old [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") in [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"),[[232]](#cite_note-245) [biogenic](/wiki/Biogenic_substance "Biogenic substance") [graphite](/wiki/Graphite "Graphite") found in 3.7 billion-year-old [metasedimentary](/wiki/Metasediment "Metasediment") rocks in [Western Greenland](/wiki/Western_Greenland "Western Greenland"),[[233]](#cite_note-246) and remains of [biotic material](/wiki/Biotic_material "Biotic material") found in 4.1 billion-year-old rocks in Western Australia.[[234]](#cite_note-247)[[235]](#cite_note-248) The [earliest direct evidence of life](/wiki/Earliest_known_life_forms "Earliest known life forms") on Earth is contained in 3.45 billion-year-old [Australian](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") rocks showing fossils of [microorganisms](/wiki/Microorganism "Microorganism").[[236]](#cite_note-249)[[237]](#cite_note-250)

During the [Neoproterozoic](/wiki/Neoproterozoic "Neoproterozoic"), 1000 to 539 Ma, much of Earth might have been covered in ice. This hypothesis has been termed "[Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth")", and it is of particular interest because it preceded the [Cambrian explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_explosion "Cambrian explosion"), when multicellular life forms significantly increased in complexity.[[238]](#cite_note-251)[[239]](#cite_note-252) Following the Cambrian explosion, 535 Ma, there have been at least five major [mass extinctions](/wiki/Extinction_event "Extinction event") and many minor ones.[[240]](#cite_note-Stanley_2016-253) Apart from the proposed current [Holocene extinction](/wiki/Holocene_extinction "Holocene extinction") event, the [most recent](/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event "Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event") was 66 Ma, when [an asteroid impact](/wiki/Chicxulub_impactor "Chicxulub impactor") triggered the extinction of non-avian dinosaurs and other large reptiles, but largely spared small animals, such as insects, [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal"), lizards, and birds. Mammalian life has diversified over the past 66 Mys, and several million years ago, an African [ape](/wiki/Ape "Ape") species gained the ability to stand upright.[[241]](#cite_note-gould1994-254)[[242]](#cite_note-255) This facilitated tool use and encouraged communication that provided the nutrition and stimulation needed for a larger brain, which led to the [evolution of humans](/wiki/Human_evolution "Human evolution"). The [development of agriculture](/wiki/History_of_agriculture "History of agriculture"), and then [civilization](/wiki/List_of_ancient_civilizations "List of ancient civilizations"), led to humans having an [influence on Earth](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment") and the nature and quantity of other life forms that continues to this day.[[243]](#cite_note-bgsa119_1_140-256)

### Challenges for life on Earth

[Extreme weather](/wiki/Extreme_weather "Extreme weather"), such as [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone"), occurs over most of Earth's surface and has a large impact on life in those areas. From 1980 to 2000, these events caused an average of 11,800 human deaths per year.[[244]](#cite_note-257) Many places are subject to [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake"), [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide"), [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami"), [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption"), [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"), [blizzards](/wiki/Blizzard "Blizzard"), [floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood"), [droughts](/wiki/Drought "Drought"), [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"), and other calamities and disasters.[[245]](#cite_note-258) [Human impact](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment") is felt in many areas due to pollution of the air and water, [acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain"), loss of vegetation ([overgrazing](/wiki/Overgrazing "Overgrazing"), [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification")), loss of wildlife, species [extinction](/wiki/Extinction "Extinction"),[[246]](#cite_note-259) [soil degradation](/wiki/Soil_degradation "Soil degradation"), [soil depletion](/wiki/Soil_depletion "Soil depletion") and [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion").[[247]](#cite_note-260) Human activities release greenhouse gases into the atmosphere which cause [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming").[[248]](#cite_note-ConsensusOnConsensus-261) This is driving [changes](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change") such as the [melting of glaciers and ice sheets](/wiki/Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850 "Retreat of glaciers since 1850"), a [global rise in average sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"), increased risk of drought and wildfires, and migration of species to colder areas.[[249]](#cite_note-Global_Warming_Effects-262)

## Earth and humans

### Human geography

Main article: [Human geography](/wiki/Human_geography "Human geography")

See also: [World](/wiki/World "World")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/BlackMarble20161km.jpg/250px-BlackMarble20161km.jpg)](/wiki/File:BlackMarble20161km.jpg)

A composite image of [artificial light emissions](/wiki/Light_pollution "Light pollution") at night on a map of Earth

Humans, who originated from earlier [primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate") in Eastern Africa 300,000 years ago, [have since been migrating](/wiki/History_of_human_migration "History of human migration") around Earth, and with the advent of agriculture in the 10th millennium BCE, have been increasingly [settling](/wiki/Sedentism "Sedentism") Earth's land.[[250]](#cite_note-263) In the 20th century, [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") became the last continent to be explored and settled by humans, although human presence there remains limited. Since the 19th century, the [human population](/wiki/World_population "World population") has grown exponentially to eight billion in the 2020s,[[251]](#cite_note-264) and is projected to peak at around ten billion in the second half of the 21st century.[[252]](#cite_note-Harvey-2020-265) Most of the growth is expected to take place in [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa").[[252]](#cite_note-Harvey-2020-265)

Distribution and [density of human population](/wiki/Population_density#Human_population_density "Population density") varies greatly around the world with the majority living in south to eastern Asia and 90% inhabiting the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") of Earth,[[253]](#cite_note-266) partly due to the [hemispherical predominance of the world's land mass](/wiki/Land_hemisphere "Land hemisphere"), with 68% of the world's land mass being in the Northern Hemisphere.[[254]](#cite_note-267) Furthermore, since the 19th century humans have increasingly converged into [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area"), with the majority living in urban areas by the 21st century.[[255]](#cite_note-OWID_urbanization_2019-268)

Beyond Earth's surface, humans have lived only in a few special-purpose deep [underground](/wiki/Underground_living "Underground living") and [underwater](/wiki/Underwater_living "Underwater living") presences and a few [space stations](/wiki/Space_station "Space station"). The human population virtually completely remains on Earth's surface, fully depending on Earth and the environment it sustains. Since the second half of the 20th century, some hundreds of humans have temporarily [stayed beyond Earth](/wiki/List_of_space_travellers_by_first_flight "List of space travellers by first flight"), a tiny fraction of whom have reached another celestial body, the Moon.[[256]](#cite_note-shayler_vis2005-269)[[257]](#cite_note-270)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Flag_of_the_United_Nations.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_United_Nations.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_Nations.svg)

The [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Nations "Flag of the United Nations") of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")

Earth has been subject to extensive human settlement, and humans have developed diverse societies and cultures. Most of Earth's land has been territorially claimed since the 19th century by [sovereign states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") (countries) separated by [political borders](/wiki/Border "Border"), and [205 such states](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states "List of sovereign states") exist today,[[258]](#cite_note-271) with only parts of Antarctica and a few small regions [remaining unclaimed](/wiki/Terra_nullius "Terra nullius").[[259]](#cite_note-272) Together, most of these states form the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the leading worldwide [intergovernmental organization](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization "Intergovernmental organization"),[[260]](#cite_note-273) which extends human governance [over the ocean](/wiki/Law_of_the_Sea "Law of the Sea") and [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctic_Treaty_System "Antarctic Treaty System"), and therefore all of Earth.

### Natural resources and land use

Main articles: [Natural resource](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource") and [Land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Global_land_use_for_food_production%2C_OWID_statistic.png/250px-Global_land_use_for_food_production%2C_OWID_statistic.png)](/wiki/File:Global_land_use_for_food_production,_OWID_statistic.png)

Earth's land use for human agriculture in 2019

Earth has resources that have been exploited by humans.[[261]](#cite_note-274) Those termed [non-renewable resources](/wiki/Non-renewable_resource "Non-renewable resource"), such as [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"), are only replenished over geological timescales.[[262]](#cite_note-275) Large deposits of fossil fuels are obtained from Earth's crust, consisting of coal, petroleum, and natural gas.[[263]](#cite_note-276) These deposits are used by humans both for energy production and as feedstock for chemical production.[[264]](#cite_note-277) Mineral [ore](/wiki/Ore "Ore") bodies have also been formed within the crust through a process of [ore genesis](/wiki/Ore_genesis "Ore genesis"), resulting from actions of [magmatism](/wiki/Magmatism "Magmatism"), erosion, and plate tectonics.[[265]](#cite_note-278) These metals and other elements are extracted by mining, a process which often causes environmental and health damage.[[266]](#cite_note-279)

Earth's biosphere produces many useful biological products for humans, including food, wood, [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceutical "Pharmaceutical"), oxygen, and the recycling of organic waste. The land-based ecosystem depends upon [topsoil](/wiki/Topsoil "Topsoil") and fresh water, and the oceanic ecosystem depends on dissolved nutrients washed down from the land.[[267]](#cite_note-science299_5607_673-280) In 2019, 39 million km2 (15 million sq mi) of Earth's land surface consisted of forest and woodlands, 12 million km2 (4.6 million sq mi) was shrub and grassland, 40 million km2 (15 million sq mi) were used for animal feed production and grazing, and 11 million km2 (4.2 million sq mi) were cultivated as croplands.[[268]](#cite_note-OWID_2019-281) Of the 12–14% of ice-free land that is used for croplands, 2 [percentage points](/wiki/Percentage_point "Percentage point") were irrigated in 2015.[[269]](#cite_note-282) Humans use natural and manufactured [building materials](/wiki/Building_material "Building material") to construct shelters and infrastructure.[[270]](#cite_note-283)

### Environmental impact

Main articles: [Human impact on the environment](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment") and [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change")

[![The graph from 1880 to 2020 shows natural drivers exhibiting fluctuations of about 0.3 degrees Celsius. Human drivers steadily increase by 0.3 degrees over 100 years to 1980, then steeply by 0.8 degrees more over the past 40 years.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Global_Temperature_And_Forces_With_Fahrenheit.svg/250px-Global_Temperature_And_Forces_With_Fahrenheit.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Global_Temperature_And_Forces_With_Fahrenheit.svg)

Change in average surface air temperature and drivers for that change. Human activity has caused increased temperatures, with natural forces adding some variability.[[271]](#cite_note-284)

Human activities have impacted Earth's environments. Through activities such as the burning of fossil fuels, humans have been increasing the amount of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") in the atmosphere, altering [Earth's energy budget](/wiki/Earth%27s_energy_budget "Earth's energy budget") and climate.[[248]](#cite_note-ConsensusOnConsensus-261)[[272]](#cite_note-285) It is estimated that global temperatures in the year 2020 were 1.2 °C (2.2 °F) warmer than the preindustrial baseline.[[273]](#cite_note-286) This increase in temperature, known as [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"), has contributed to the [melting of glaciers](/wiki/Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850 "Retreat of glaciers since 1850"), [rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"), increased risk of drought and wildfires, and migration of species to colder areas.[[249]](#cite_note-Global_Warming_Effects-262)

The concept of [planetary boundaries](/wiki/Planetary_boundaries "Planetary boundaries") was introduced to quantify humanity's impact on Earth. Of the nine identified boundaries, five have been crossed: [Biosphere integrity](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss"), climate change, chemical pollution, destruction of wild habitats and the [nitrogen cycle](/wiki/Nitrogen_cycle "Nitrogen cycle") are thought to have passed the safe threshold.[[274]](#cite_note-Boundaries-287)[[275]](#cite_note-288) As of 2018, no country meets the basic needs of its population without transgressing planetary boundaries. It is thought possible to provide all basic physical needs globally within sustainable levels of resource use.[[276]](#cite_note-289)

### Cultural and historical viewpoint

Main articles: [Earth in culture](/wiki/Earth_in_culture "Earth in culture") and [Earth in science fiction](/wiki/Earth_in_science_fiction "Earth in science fiction")

[Human cultures](/wiki/Culture "Culture") have developed many views of the planet.[[277]](#cite_note-NYT-20181224b-290) The standard [astronomical symbols](/wiki/Astronomical_symbols "Astronomical symbols") of Earth are a quartered circle, [![🜨](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Earth_symbol_%28fixed_width%29.svg/20px-Earth_symbol_%28fixed_width%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Earth_symbol_(fixed_width).svg "🜨"),[[278]](#cite_note-liungman2004-291) representing the [four corners of the world](/wiki/Four_corners_of_the_world "Four corners of the world"), and a [globus cruciger](/wiki/Globus_cruciger "Globus cruciger"), [![♁](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Globus_cruciger_%28fixed_width%29.svg/20px-Globus_cruciger_%28fixed_width%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Globus_cruciger_(fixed_width).svg "♁"). Earth is sometimes [personified](/wiki/Personification "Personification") as a [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity"). In many cultures, it is a [mother goddess](/wiki/Mother_goddess "Mother goddess") that is also the primary [fertility deity](/wiki/Fertility_deity "Fertility deity").[[279]](#cite_note-Stookey-2004-292) [Creation myths](/wiki/Creation_myth "Creation myth") in many religions involve the creation of Earth by a supernatural deity or deities.[[279]](#cite_note-Stookey-2004-292) The [Gaia hypothesis](/wiki/Gaia_hypothesis "Gaia hypothesis"), developed in the mid-20th century, compared Earth's environments and life as a single self-regulating organism leading to broad stabilization of the conditions of habitability.[[280]](#cite_note-293)[[281]](#cite_note-294)[[282]](#cite_note-295)

[![Photograph of Earth taken by the Apollo 17 mission. The Arabian peninsula, Africa and Madagascar lie in the upper half of the disc, whereas Antarctica is at the bottom.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/The_Blue_Marble%2C_AS17-148-22727.jpg/250px-The_Blue_Marble%2C_AS17-148-22727.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Blue_Marble,_AS17-148-22727.jpg)

*[The Blue Marble](/wiki/The_Blue_Marble "The Blue Marble")*, [Apollo 17](/wiki/Apollo_17 "Apollo 17"), December 1972

[Images of Earth taken from space](/wiki/Timeline_of_first_images_of_Earth_from_space "Timeline of first images of Earth from space"), particularly during the Apollo program, have been credited with altering the way that people viewed the planet that they lived on, called the [overview effect](/wiki/Overview_effect "Overview effect"), emphasizing its beauty, uniqueness and apparent fragility.[[283]](#cite_note-296)[[284]](#cite_note-297) In particular, this caused a realization of the scope of effects from human activity on Earth's environment. Enabled by science, particularly [Earth observation](/wiki/Earth_observation "Earth observation"),[[285]](#cite_note-298) humans have started to take [action on environmental issues](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism") globally,[[286]](#cite_note-299) acknowledging the impact of humans and the [interconnectedness of Earth's environments](/wiki/Ecological_network "Ecological network").[[287]](#cite_note-300)

Scientific investigation has resulted in several culturally transformative shifts in people's view of the planet. Initial belief in a [flat Earth](/wiki/Flat_Earth "Flat Earth") was gradually displaced in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") by the idea of a [spherical Earth](/wiki/Spherical_Earth "Spherical Earth"), which was attributed to both the philosophers [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras") and [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides").[[288]](#cite_note-301)[[289]](#cite_note-302) Earth was generally believed to be [the center of the universe](/wiki/Geocentrism "Geocentrism")[[290]](#cite_note-303) until the 16th century, when scientists first concluded that it was [a moving object](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism"), one of the planets of the Solar System.[[291]](#cite_note-arnett20060716-304)

It was only during the 19th century that geologists realized [Earth's age](/wiki/Earth%27s_age "Earth's age") was at least many millions of years.[[292]](#cite_note-305) [Lord Kelvin](/wiki/Lord_Kelvin "Lord Kelvin") used [thermodynamics](/wiki/Thermodynamics "Thermodynamics") to estimate the age of Earth to be between 20 million and 400 million years in 1864, sparking a vigorous debate on the subject; it was only when radioactivity and [radioactive dating](/wiki/Radiometric_dating "Radiometric dating") were discovered in the late 19th and early 20th centuries that a reliable mechanism for determining Earth's age was established, proving the planet to be billions of years old.[[293]](#cite_note-306)[[294]](#cite_note-307)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-epoch_1-0)** All astronomical quantities vary, both [secularly](/wiki/Secular_phenomena "Secular phenomena") and [periodically](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency"). The quantities given are the values at the instant [J2000.0](/wiki/J2000.0 "J2000.0") of the secular variation, ignoring all periodic variations.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-apsis_2-0)** aphelion = *a* × (1 + *e*); perihelion = *a* × (1 – *e*), where *a* is the semi-major axis and *e* is the eccentricity. The difference between Earth's perihelion and aphelion is 5 million kilometers. —Wilkinson, John (2009). *Probing the New Solar System*. CSIRO Publishing. p. 144. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-643-09949-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-643-09949-4 "Special:BookSources/978-0-643-09949-4").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-circ_13-0)** Earth's [circumference](/wiki/Circumference "Circumference") is almost exactly 40,000 km because the meter was calibrated on this measurement—more specifically, 1/10-millionth of the distance between the poles and the equator.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-surfacecover_15-0)** Due to natural fluctuations, ambiguities surrounding [ice shelves](/wiki/Ice_shelf "Ice shelf"), and mapping conventions for [vertical datums](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum"), exact values for land and ocean coverage are not meaningful. Based on data from the [Vector Map](/wiki/Vector_Map "Vector Map") and [Global Landcover](http://www.landcover.org/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20150326085837/http://www.landcover.org/) 26 March 2015 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") datasets, extreme values for coverage of lakes and streams are 0.6% and 1.0% of Earth's surface. The ice sheets of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") are counted as land, even though much of the rock that supports them lies below sea level.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** Source for minimum,[[18]](#cite_note-asu_lowest_temp-22) mean,[[19]](#cite_note-23) and maximum[[20]](#cite_note-asu_highest_temp-24) surface temperature
6. **[^](#cite_ref-77)**  If Earth were shrunk to the size of a [billiard ball](/wiki/Billiard_ball "Billiard ball"), some areas of Earth such as large mountain ranges and oceanic trenches would feel like tiny imperfections, whereas much of the planet, including the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") and the [abyssal plains](/wiki/Abyssal_plain "Abyssal plain"), would feel smoother.[[71]](#cite_note-76)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-85)** Locally varies between 5 and 200 km.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-86)** Locally varies between 5 and 70 km.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-jaes41_3_379_137-0)** Including the [Somali Plate](/wiki/Somali_Plate "Somali Plate"), which is being formed out of the African Plate. See: Chorowicz, Jean (October 2005). "The East African rift system". *[Journal of African Earth Sciences](/wiki/Journal_of_African_Earth_Sciences "Journal of African Earth Sciences")*. **43** (1–3): 379–410. [Bibcode](/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier) "Bibcode (identifier)"):[2005JAfES..43..379C](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2005JAfES..43..379C). [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1016/j.jafrearsci.2005.07.019](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.jafrearsci.2005.07.019).
10. **[^](#cite_ref-Aoki_196-0)** The ultimate source of these figures, uses the term "seconds of UT1" instead of "seconds of mean solar time". —Aoki, S.; Kinoshita, H.; Guinot, B.; Kaplan, G. H.; McCarthy, D. D.; Seidelmann, P. K. (1982). "The new definition of universal time". *Astronomy and Astrophysics*. **105** (2): 359–361. [Bibcode](/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier) "Bibcode (identifier)"):[1982A&A...105..359A](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1982A&A...105..359A).
11. **[^](#cite_ref-solar_energy_208-0)** Aphelion is 103.4% of the distance to perihelion. Due to the inverse square law, the radiation at perihelion is about 106.9% of the energy at aphelion.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-hill_radius_212-0)** For Earth, the [Hill radius](/wiki/Hill_radius "Hill radius") is ![{\displaystyle R_{H}=a\left({\frac {m}{3M}}\right)^{\frac {1}{3}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/596f8baf206a81478afd4194b44138715dc1a05c), where *m* is the mass of Earth, *a* is an astronomical unit, and *M* is the mass of the Sun. So the radius in AU is about ![{\displaystyle \left({\frac {1}{3\cdot 332,946}}\right)^{\frac {1}{3}}=0.01}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7ab5412b4744a2163206868fe8ec2726455c4785).
13. **[^](#cite_ref-space_debris_233-0)** As of 4 January 2018, the United States Strategic Command tracked a total of 18,835 artificial objects, mostly debris. See: Anz-Meador, Phillip; Shoots, Debi, eds. (February 2018). ["Satellite Box Score"](https://orbitaldebris.jsc.nasa.gov/quarterly-news/pdfs/odqnv22i1.pdf) (PDF). *Orbital Debris Quarterly News*. **22** (1): 12. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20190402034308/https://orbitaldebris.jsc.nasa.gov/quarterly-news/pdfs/odqnv22i1.pdf) (PDF) from the original on 2 April 2019. Retrieved 18 April 2018.
